title: Wasserstein Space of Quantum Chaos
authors: Koji Hashimoto, Norihiro Tanahashi, Kentaroh Yoshida
abstract: We find that the effective dimension of the Wasserstein space of energy eigenstates decreases as a quantum system becomes more chaotic. To demonstrate this, we study a quantum coupled harmonic oscillator system using Husimi Q-representations, to which Sinkhorn-regularized optimal transport is applied to construct an embedding geometry via the Gram-spectrum method. We also demonstrate that exponential OTOC growth, referred to here as quantum scrambling even in the absence of chaos, induces a folding structure in the emergent Wasserstein space, which may underlie the chaotic reduction of the Wasserstein dimension. At the separatrix (the scrambling point) of the inverted harmonic oscillator, the Wasserstein distance correctly captures the Lyapunov exponent. Furthermore, we discover that a branching structure in the Wasserstein space signals quantum scar states within the chaotic sea of phase space. Our optimal transport approach thus provides a new diagnostic for quantum chaos, quantum scrambling, quantum scars, and quantum Lyapunov exponents. The observed chaotic dimensional reduction also supports the recent conjecture [arXiv:2604.17649] that the Wasserstein space serves as an emergent holographic space through the manifold hypothesis, since chaoticity is a characteristic signature of black holes in holography.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the geometry of the Wasserstein space of quantum states undergoes a dimensional reduction as a system becomes chaotic. By showing that quantum scrambling induces a folding structure in this space, the authors propose a new diagnostic for chaos and support the idea that holography emerges from such dimensional reduction.
main_problem: Investigating the relationship between quantum chaos, quantum scrambling, and the geometric structure of the Wasserstein space of energy eigenstates.
main_result: The effective dimension of the Wasserstein space decreases as a quantum system becomes more chaotic, a process driven by a 'folding' structure induced by quantum scrambling.
method: The authors use Sinkhorn-regularized optimal transport on Husimi Q-representations and apply the Gram-spectrum method with Multidimensional Scaling (MGS) to embed the Wasserstein distance into Euclidean space.
model_or_system: The study utilizes several models: 1D potentials (harmonic oscillator, double-well, and partly flattened oscillator) and a 2D coupled harmonic oscillator with a quartic mixing term controlled by a coupling constant g.
key_observables: Effective dimension of Wasserstein space, Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlators (OTOC), Lyapunov exponent, and Gram-matrix eigenvalue decay constant B.
important_parameters: Coupling constant g (controlling integrability to chaos), Sinkhorn regularization parameter epsilon, and phase-space grid resolution.
main_assumptions: The results are based on truncated harmonic oscillator bases and discretized phase-space grids; the authors note that higher energy levels may introduce more complex non-Euclidean geometry.
figures_summary: Figures show OTOC growth (scrambling), potential energy profiles, Husimi Q-representations in phase space, Wasserstein distance heatmaps, and 2D MDS embeddings showing line vs. folded structures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dimensional reduction, presents 1D models to demonstrate scrambling-induced folding, analyzes the 2D coupled oscillator to show chaotic dimensional reduction, and concludes with a discussion on the holographic manifold hypothesis and numerical robustness.
why_interesting: It provides a new geometric language (optimal transport) to unify diverse phenomena like quantum scars, Lyapunov exponents, and scrambling, while offering a potential geometric mechanism for the emergence of holographic spacetime.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The time evolution of the microcanonical out-of-time-order correlators (OTOCs) for the double-well case (a) and the partly flattened oscillator case (b). In both cases (a) and (b), the red lines grow exponentially in time, which is the scrambling phenomenon. For (a), the plotted one is for n = 0 (blue), n = 2 (green), n = 5 (red) and n = 9 (black), where n = 0, 1, 2, · · · labels the energy level of even parity states counted from the ground state. The n = 5 state has E = 0.088, which is close to the top of the potential hill. For (b), the plotted one is for n = 0 (blue), n = 2 (green), n = 4 (red) and n = 9 (black). The n = 4 state has E = 13.07, which is close to the plateau...
Figure 2. One-dimensional potentials used in this section: harmonic, double-well, and partly flattened oscillator.
Figure 3. Husimi Q-representation for 16 energy eigenstates: harmonic oscillator. From top-left to bottom right, the energy grows, and we labels them as n = 0, 1, · · · , 15.
Figure 4. Husimi Q-representation for 16 energy eigenstates: double-well.
Figure 5. Husimi Q-representation for 16 energy eigenstates: partly flattened oscillator.
